text.title: CQRS Pattern Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding CQRS Pattern. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. The {skip-this-methodology} rule routes inapplicable cases.

rule.statement: Commands and Queries MUST use different models (data shapes). Commands accept a command object and persist via the write model; queries return DTOs derived from a read-model projection. Sharing a single model across both is a CQRS violation.

rationale.source: Greg Young, CQRS (2010); Vaughn Vernon, Implementing DDD (2013)

rule.rationale: Reusing one model forces it to satisfy both write (validation, invariants) and read (projection, joins) needs. CQRS's value is the freedom to optimize each separately.

rule.statement: Commands MUST return void or the created entity's ID — never the persisted entity or projection. Clients that need to see the result perform a separate query.

rationale.source: Greg Young CQRS docs; event-sourcing patterns

rule.rationale: Returning the persisted state couples write and read paths and breaks eventual consistency (the client expects the data to be immediately readable, which only holds under strong consistency).

rule.statement: Queries MUST NOT mutate state, increment counters, write logs by side effect, or send commands. Queries are pure functions of the read model.
rule.rationale: A query that mutates state cannot be retried safely or cached. Side-effecting queries undermine the read-side caching strategy CQRS depends on.

rule.statement: Read models MUST be built by projections that consume domain events from the write side. Projections run as separate processes/consumers; they catch up from the event log on restart.

rationale.source: Greg Young, Event Sourcing (2010); Kafka Streams projections

rule.rationale: Hand-maintained read models drift from the write side. Event-sourced projections give a single source of truth and replayability.

rule.statement: When a query has materially different access patterns (search, leaderboard, dashboard), build a SEPARATE read model. A single read model serving every query becomes a kitchen sink and erodes the CQRS payoff.

rationale.source: CQRS in practice (Microsoft Azure architecture docs)

rule.rationale: Multiple optimized read models is the central benefit of CQRS — give up on a single shared one early.

rule.statement: Skip CQRS when the system is a simple CRUD app, the team has no need for read-side scaling, and the domain has no behaviour worth modelling.
rule.rationale: CQRS adds complexity (write model, read model, projection, eventual consistency). CRUD apps pay the cost without benefit.
